one_line_takeaway: Unverified machine-unlearning requests are a denial-of-service vector: an adversary can submit a visually innocuous 'forget set' that, once unlearned, collapses model accuracy to near-zero, and current hash/embedding verifiers cannot filter these stealthy requests without rejecting legitimate ones.
key_finding: GA-based unlearning is extremely fragile to adversarial forget sets. White-box attacks push the unlearned model's absolute test accuracy down to 3.6% on CIFAR-10 and 0.4% on ImageNet; black-box attacks reach 8.5% (CIFAR-10) and 1.3% (ImageNet). The perturbations are visually near-invisible (Fig 2: CIFAR-10 retain accuracy collapses from 99.44% to 4.32%; ImageNet 96.41% to 0.04%). The attack survives every relaxation: it transfers across models and across unlearning algorithms (~90% retain drop even without knowing the exact algorithm, Table 5), works from CIFAR-100 or random-pixel initialization when the attacker has no training data (Table 6), and defeats detection -- six hash/embedding verification schemes have AUROC > 0.7 on unbounded attacks but drop BELOW 0.5 under l2-bounded stealthy attacks (Fig 4), i.e., worse than chance, while still causing 84.1% retain drop. Even the strongest proposed defense (similarity-search indexing that forces requests onto real training images) is bypassed by adversarial SELECTION of valid examples: 10 cherry-picked real forget images raise retain error to 30.9% vs 3.8% for a random forget set (Fig 5).
core_contribution: The paper exposes and weaponizes a previously overlooked assumption in machine-unlearning protocols: that the forget set requested for removal is actually a subset of the training set (Dforget subset of Dtrain). The authors show that when this assumption is unverified, an adversary posing as a data owner can submit an 'adversarially crafted' forget set that, once processed by a standard gradient-ascent-based unlearning algorithm, catastrophically destroys the resulting model's accuracy on data it was NOT supposed to affect (the retain/holdout sets). Concrete own contributions: (1) a formal threat model for a data-owner-side adversary whose goal is performance degradation (general or targeted, optionally stealthy); (2) two attack algorithms -- a white-box attack that back-propagates the retain loss THROUGH the unlearning update via gradient-through-gradient / Hessian-vector products (Algorithm 1), and a black-box attack using zeroth-order gradient estimation with query-only loss access (Algorithm 2); (3) an empirical study answering 'what goes wrong' (Q1) and 'can it be robustly detected' (Q2), showing that six verification schemes fail against stealthy attacks; (4) a theoretical existence proof (Appendix C) that in a linear-model setting, GA-like unlearning on a slightly perturbed forget set misclassifies all retain examples.
threat_model: Adversary is a data owner who legitimately submits unlearning ('right to be forgotten') requests to a model deployer. Goal: craft a forget set Dadv_forget so that after the deployer runs unlearning U(ftarget, Dadv_forget), the unlearned model f_adv_unlearn suffers large accuracy loss on the retain set Dretain and held-out data -- degradation is either general (all classes) or targeted (a chosen class, backdoor-style), optionally stealthy (crafted images stay visually/l2-close to real training images to evade detection). Capabilities: attacker holds a subset of Dtrain and knows the unlearning algorithm (both assumptions later relaxed in Sec 5 with the attack still working). Access is split into white-box (full model access, can back-prop through the model and the unlearning update) and black-box (query-only access to the model's loss on chosen inputs). Trust failure exploited: the deployer never verifies that submitted forget examples are genuinely in the training set, and even if it stores hashes/embeddings, stealthy perturbations evade the check.
limitations: Scope is image classification only; the authors flag transfer to language-model unlearning as future work. The attack targets only GA-based (differentiable) unlearning methods -- non-differentiable approaches such as task vectors are untested and the theory covers only a stylized linear model. Effectiveness is size-dependent: white-box attacks are strong across sizes but with high variance at very small forget sets (|Dforget| <= 10, e.g., ImageNet size-10 mean drop only 31.98% with std 26.79), while black-box attacks degrade for larger forget sets due to zeroth-order optimization scaling poorly in high dimensions. Non-training-data-initialized attacks (random pixels / CIFAR-100) are weaker and the authors concede they would likely be caught by the detectors. Real-world urgency is tempered by the authors' own ethics note that machine unlearning is not yet widely deployed. Note: Sec 3.3 text cites 'up to 89.6% drop on CIFAR-10' for black-box, which is higher than the max in Table 4 (42.06% at size 10), suggesting the headline black-box CIFAR figure may come from a different configuration/appendix than the main table.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (target: ResNet-18 from the NeurIPS 2023 Machine Unlearning Challenge)
CIFAR-100 (used only as non-training initialization for the attack)
Random-pixel inputs (used as non-training initialization for the attack)
quantitative_results: White-box attack final absolute test accuracy: 3.6% on CIFAR-10, 0.4% on ImageNet
Black-box attack final absolute test accuracy: 8.5% on CIFAR-10, 1.3% on ImageNet
White-box retain-accuracy DROP (Table 1) up to 95.45% on CIFAR-10 (size 100) and 96.88% on ImageNet (size 100)
Figure 2 case: CIFAR-10 retain accuracy 99.44% -> 4.32%; ImageNet 96.41% -> 0.04% after adversarial forget set
Cross-algorithm transfer (Table 5, |Dforget|=20, white-box): ~90-95% retain drop even when attacker's assumed algorithm differs from the deployed one (e.g. GA->GAGDR 93.52%, GAKLR->GA 93.52%)
White-box generalization across methods (Table 3): retain drop up to 94.01% (CIFAR-10 GAKLR size 20) and 96.79% (ImageNet GAKLR size 50)
Non-training initialization (Table 6, CIFAR-10 retain drop, black-box): random pixels 67.14% and CIFAR-100 30.69% at size 10, decaying to 20.89% / 8.63% at size 100
Transferability shadow->target (Table 2, CIFAR-10 retain drop): 68.04% max at size 10, decaying to 3.91% at size 100
Detection AUROC (Fig 4): non-stealthy attack detectors mostly >0.7 (LPIPS up to 1.00); under l2-bounded stealthy attack (retain drop 84.1%) AUROC drops below 0.5, LPIPS collapses to ~0.00
Indexing-defense bypass (Fig 5): adversarially selected valid forget set of 10 examples -> 30.9% retain error vs 3.8% for a random forget set
baselines_compared: Benign unlearned model funlearn vs adversarial f_adv_unlearn (accuracy-drop baseline)
Three unlearning algorithms as attack targets: Gradient Ascent (GA), GA+Gradient-Descent-on-Retain (GAGDR), GA+KL-on-Retain (GAKLR)
Six verification/detection schemes: average hashing, perceptual hashing, difference hashing, wavelet hashing, LPIPS embedding, and pixel l2 distance
Similarity-search / indexing unlearning protocol (proposed stronger defense) vs simple distance checking
